## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *Differentiate between a "reference method" and a "standard method" in food analysis. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reference Method | Standard Method |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fully validated, internationally recognized gold-standard procedure published by authoritative bodies (AOAC, ISO, Codex) | Routine procedure used for regulatory compliance, may be derived from reference or be a reference itself |
| Purpose | Method validation, dispute resolution, calibration of alternative methods | Day-to-day QC and regulatory testing |
| Example | **ISO 4833-1:2013** (aerobic plate count at 30 °C) for total viable count | **FSSAI-prescribed** total plate count protocol in a food-testing lab |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for correct example.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *List the AOAC/ISO acceptance criteria for validation of an alternative microbiological method against a reference method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The acceptance criteria per **ISO 16140-2 / AOAC OMA** are:
1. **Sensitivity** $\geq 90\%$ (true positive rate)
2. **Specificity** $\geq 90\%$ (true negative rate)
3. **False-negative rate** $\leq 5\%$ (critical for pathogens)
4. **False-positive rate** $\leq 10\%$
5. **Overall accuracy** $\geq 90\%$
6. **Limit of Detection (LOD)** equal to or lower than the reference method

*(½ mark each for any 6 of the above criteria.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the classical (reference) methods used for chemical analysis of foods. Discuss gravimetric, titrimetric, and colorimetric techniques with suitable food-science examples. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Compare the classical methods with modern instrumental techniques (HPLC, GC, AAS, ICP-MS) on the basis of sensitivity, time, cost, and regulatory acceptance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classical (Reference) Chemical Methods (7 marks):**

Classical methods form the bedrock of food composition analysis and are codified in **AOAC Official Methods of Analysis**.

1. **Gravimetric Analysis** (2 marks)
   - Principle: isolation of the analyte as a pure, stable, weighable compound.
   - *Example 1*: Moisture determination — oven drying at **$103 \pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$** to constant mass (AOAC 950.46). Mass loss = water content.
   - *Example 2*: Ash determination — incineration in muffle furnace at **$550^{\circ}\text{C}$** (AOAC 920.153) until carbon-free; residue is weighed.
   - *Example 3*: Crude fiber determination (AOAC 962.09) — acid/alkali digestion of defatted sample, gravimetric quantification of residue.

2. **Titrimetric (Volumetric) Analysis** (2 marks)
   - Principle: stoichiometric reaction of analyte with a titrant of known concentration; endpoint via indicator or electrode.
   - *Example 1*: **Kjeldahl method** for total nitrogen $\rightarrow$ crude protein (AOAC 991.20). Digestion $\rightarrow$ distillation $\rightarrow$ boric acid capture $\rightarrow$ back-titration with HCl.
   - *Example 2*: Acid value, peroxide value, saponification value of oils and fats.
   - *Example 3*: Salt (NaCl) determination by Mohr's method (AgNO₃ titration with K₂CrO₄ indicator).

3. **Colorimetric / Spectrophotometric Methods** (2 marks)
   - Principle: reaction of analyte with a chromogenic reagent to form a coloured product; absorbance measured per Beer-Lambert law.
   - *Example 1*: Phosphorus by **molybdenum blue method** (AOAC 995.11) — absorbance at 660 nm.
   - *Example 2*: Iron by **phenanthroline method** (AOAC 944.02).
   - *Example 3*: Aflatoxins via **bifunctional clean-up column + bromine derivatization**, fluorescence measurement at 360/440 nm (AOAC 990.33).

4. **Sensory / Physical Tests** (1 mark)
   - pH, titratable acidity, refractive index (Brix), colourimetry (Lovibond, Hunter Lab), viscosity.

**(b) Comparison: Classical vs. Instrumental (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Classical (Reference) | Instrumental (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity / LOD** | 1–10 ppm (mg/kg) | 1–10 ppb (μg/kg) to ppt (ng/kg) |
| **Specificity** | Moderate; prone to interferences | High; coupled with MS, FTIR detectors |
| **Time per Analysis** | 4–8 hours (digestion, distillation) | 15–60 min per sample (with autosampler) |
| **Sample Volume** | 5–50 g | 1–2 g; microliter-scale injection |
| **Cost per Test** | Low (₹100–500) | High (₹1000–10000) |
| **Equipment Cost** | Minimal (burette, balance) | High (HPLC ₹25 Lakh; ICP-MS ₹1 Cr) |
| **Skill Required** | Skilled analyst (glassware handling) | Specialist trained in instrumentation |
| **Regulatory Acceptance** | Universally accepted (AOAC, ISO) | Accepted after cross-validation against reference |
| **Automation** | Mostly manual | High automation; 24/7 operation |
| **Examples** | Kjeldahl, oven moisture, Mohr's titration | HPLC for vitamins, GC for pesticides, AAS for lead |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Classical methods remain indispensable for validation, calibration, and dispute resolution. Modern instruments offer speed, sensitivity, and multi-analyte capability but require rigorous validation against the classical reference before regulatory acceptance.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *(a)* Discuss the reference methods for microbiological analysis of foods as per ISO and AOAC. Describe the Standard Plate Count, MPN, and selective plating techniques in detail. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Critically compare the conventional culture-based methods with newer rapid methods (PCR, ELISA, ATP bioluminescence, FTIR) in terms of time, sensitivity, cost, and regulatory status. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Reference Microbiological Methods (7 marks):**

1. **Standard Plate Count (SPC) / Aerobic Plate Count (APC)** (2.5 marks)
   - **Standard**: ISO 4833-1:2013 / AOAC 966.23
   - **Procedure**: A food sample (25 g or 25 mL) is homogenized in 225 mL buffered peptone water. Serial 10-fold dilutions prepared. 1 mL (or 0.1 mL) plated in duplicate on **Plate Count Agar (PCA)**. Incubate at **$30 \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 72 ± 3 h**.
   - **Counting**: Count plates with 30–300 colonies. Compute as $\text{CFU/g}$.
   - **Limit**: Cannot detect stressed/injured cells, slow-growers, or viable-but-non-culturable (VBNC) cells.

2. **Most Probable Number (MPN)** (2.5 marks)
   - **Standard**: ISO 21528-1 (presumptive *Enterobacteriaceae*).
   - **Procedure**: Three or five replicates of three serial dilutions inoculated into selective broth (e.g., Lauryl Sulphate Tryptose for coliforms). Tubes scored +/− for gas/turbidity after 24–48 h. Statistical probability table consulted.
   - **Use**: When samples contain low counts, particulates, or are unsuitable for plating.

3. **Selective Plating and Biochemical Confirmation** (2 marks)
   - *Salmonella* — ISO 6579: pre-enrichment (BPW, 18 h) → selective enrichment (RVS/MSRV) → plating (XLD, BGA) → biochemical (TSI, LIA) → serological confirmation.
   - *S. aureus* — ISO 6888: Baird-Parker agar + egg yolk + tellurite; coagulase test.
   - *Listeria monocytogenes* — ISO 11290: half-Fraser + Fraser broths, Oxford & PALCAM agars, CAMP test, hemolysis.

**(b) Comparison: Conventional vs. Rapid Methods (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Conventional (ISO/AOAC) | Rapid (PCR/ELISA/ATP/FTIR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time** | 3–7 days | 15 min – 6 h |
| **LOD** | 1–10 CFU/g (post-enrichment) | 1–100 CFU/25 g (post-enrichment) |
| **Specificity** | High (biochemical + serology) | Very high (DNA or antigen-specific) |
| **Viable Cell Detection** | Yes (only grows if alive) | Variable (PCR amplifies DNA from dead cells) |
| **Skill** | Trained microbiologist | Semi-skilled kits; specialist for molecular |
| **Cost per Test** | ₹200–1000 | ₹1000–5000 |
| **Equipment** | Basic lab infrastructure | Thermocycler, luminometer, FTIR (high capex) |
| **Throughput** | Limited by incubator space | 96-well plates, automated |
| **Regulatory Status** | Gold standard, universally accepted | Accepted only after ISO 16140 validation |
| **False Positives** | Low | Higher (cross-reactivity, dead-cell DNA) |
| **Confirmatory Need** | Not needed (definitive) | Yes — positives must be confirmed by reference |
| **Best Use Case** | Regulatory compliance, exports, outbreak confirmation | In-process QC, high-throughput screening, HACCP verification |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Rapid methods are powerful screening tools but cannot (yet) entirely replace reference culture methods. The current paradigm is a **two-tier workflow**: rapid screen → confirm positives by reference. Future direction is multi-omics (metagenomics, proteomics) integrated with AI-driven pipelines.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "reference" and "standard" methods** — they are *not* the same. Reference = gold standard for validation; standard = routine procedure. Examiners will deduct 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the official body** (ISO number / AOAC number) — always cite the method code (e.g., ISO 4833-1:2013, AOAC 950.46) to score full marks.
> 3. **Omitting the matrix in examples** — a method applied to "milk" is different from one applied to "meat"; examiners expect the matrix mentioned.
> 4. **Tabulation errors** in the comparison table — keep parameters **consistent on both sides** (don't write "fast" on one side and "time" on the other).
> 5. **Ignoring regulatory caveats** — a rapid method answer that does NOT mention "ISO 16140 validation" is incomplete; that one line can fetch 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Missing units and numerical examples** — including numbers (e.g., "30 °C / 72 h", "CFU/g", "1 CFU/25 g") elevates the answer from descriptive to analytical.
> 7. **One-sided discussion** in the comparison — always discuss BOTH advantages AND limitations of the rapid methods.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**
>
> **1. Reference Methods — Key Facts**
> - Defined and published by AOAC, ISO, BIS, FSSAI, Codex, USFDA BAM.
> - Chemical: gravimetric, titrimetric, colorimetric (AOAC 950.46 moisture, 991.20 Kjeldahl, 920.153 ash).
> - Microbiological: ISO 4833 (SPC), ISO 21528 (MPN/Enterobacteriaceae), ISO 6579 (Salmonella), ISO 6888 (S. aureus), ISO 11290 (Listeria).
> - **Time**: 2–7 days for microbiology; 4–8 h for chemical.
> - Universally accepted by regulators; serve as gold standard for validation.
>
> **2. Rapid Methods — Key Facts**
> - **PCR/qPCR** (BAX System, GeneDisc) — molecular detection of pathogen DNA; 4–6 h; highly specific.
> - **ELISA** (Tecra, Transia) — antigen detection; 2–4 h; semi-quantitative.
> - **ATP Bioluminescence** (3M Clean-Trace) — indicates surface hygiene; 30 seconds; *no speciation*.
> - **FTIR / NIR** — chemical fingerprinting; 1–5 min; non-destructive.
> - **HPLC / GC-MS** — chemical contaminants; minutes; multi-analyte.
> - **MALDI-TOF** (VITEK MS) — microbial ID from colony; 10 min from positive culture.
> - **Lateral Flow Devices (LFD)** — strip tests; 5–15 min; qualitative.
> - **Flow Cytometry / Impedance** — automated total counts; 1–3 h.
> - **Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS)** — outbreak fingerprinting; days; cutting-edge.
>
> **3. Validation Framework (ISO 16140-2)**
> - Sensitivity $\geq 90\%$, Specificity $\geq 90\%$, FNR $\leq 5\%$, FPR $\leq 10\%$, Accuracy $\geq 90\%$.
> - Inter-laboratory study, inclusivity/exclusivity, robustness required.
>
> **4. Critical Equations to Memorize**
> - Beer-Lambert: $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$
> - Protein factor: $\text{Protein} = N \times 6.25$ (or specific factor)
> - CFU/g formula, MPN table lookup, LOD = $3.3\sigma/S$, LOQ = $10\sigma/S$.
> - Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN), Specificity = TN/(TN+FP).
>
> **5. Industry Buzzwords the Examiner Loves**
> - "ISO 16140 validation"
> - "Two-tier screening and confirmation"
> - "HACCP verification"
> - "AOAC Official Method of Analysis (OMA)"
> - "VBNC cells and DNA persistence after cell death"
> - "PCR inhibitors in food matrices (polyphenols, fats)"
>
> **6. One-Line Facts That Fetch Marks**
> - The *Salmonella* standard (ISO 6579) takes 5 days; PCR can do it in 6 h.
> - Kjeldahl factor for milk is 6.38; for wheat it is 5.70; default is 6.25.
> - PCA incubation is $30 \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $72 \pm 3$ h; Baird-Parker is $37^{\circ}\text{C}$ for $24$–$48$ h.
> - FTIR does not destroy the sample; useful for rapid adulteration screening.
> - Real-time PCR can quantify cells, but cannot distinguish live from dead without **EMA/PMA pre-treatment**.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Role of Food Quality and Safety Evaluation

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Quality Evaluation** is the systematic, science-based process of measuring, analyzing, and judging the inherent characteristics of a food product against predefined standards, specifications, and consumer expectations. It encompasses **physical, chemical, microbiological, and sensory attributes** that determine a product's acceptability, shelf-life, and market value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Safety Evaluation** is the rigorous scientific assessment of a food product to ensure it is free from physical, chemical, and biological hazards that could cause harm to the consumer. It operates within the framework of **HACCP (Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point)**, **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** regulations, and **Codex Alimentarius** international standards.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of food quality and safety evaluation like a **hospital's diagnostic process for a patient**:
- **Safety evaluation** is the *critical, life-saving check* (like an ECG or blood test) — it asks: *"Will this food harm anyone?"*
- **Quality evaluation** is the *comprehensive wellness check* (like a full-body health assessment) — it asks: *"Is this food appealing, nutritious, and worth buying again?"*

Just as a doctor uses multiple tools (stethoscope, blood tests, MRI), food scientists use **multiple evaluation techniques** — chemical assays, microbial counts, texture analyzers, and human sensory panels — to build a complete picture of a food product.

### The Three Pillars of Food Evaluation

| Pillar | Primary Concern | Key Question Answered |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality** | Desirability | "Is it good enough to buy?" |
| **Safety** | Harmlessness | "Will it cause illness or injury?" |
| **Authenticity** | Wholesomeness | "Is it genuine and unadulterated?" |

### Standard Metrics & Regulatory Bodies

> [!NOTE]
> - **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)**: Expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**
> - **Maximum Residue Limits (MRL)**: Expressed in **ppm (parts per million)** or **ppb (parts per billion)**
> - **Total Plate Count (TPC)**: Expressed in **CFU/g (Colony Forming Units per gram)**
> - **Water Activity (a_w)**: Dimensionless value, range **0.0 to 1.0**
> - **Key Regulatory Bodies**: **FSSAI** (India), **FDA** (USA), **EFSA** (Europe), **Codex Alimentarius** (International)

---

## 1.2 Sensory Quality — Definition and Scope

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory Quality** refers to those characteristics of a food product that are perceived and evaluated by the **five human senses** — *sight (vision), hearing (audition), touch (tactile), smell (olfaction), and taste (gustation)*. **Sensory Evaluation** is the scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to food products as perceived by these senses.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine walking into a bakery. Before you even taste the bread, you **see** its golden crust, **smell** its yeasty aroma, **hear** the crunch when the baker taps it, **feel** its crusty exterior, and then **taste** its soft interior. Your brain instantly combines all these sensory inputs into a single judgment: *"This is fresh, high-quality bread."* 

**Sensory evaluation is the formal, scientific version of this everyday experience** — converting subjective human perception into objective, analyzable data using standardized protocols.

### The Five Sensory Modalities in Food

| Sense | Organ | Detected Attributes | Example in Food |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vision** | Eyes | Color, Size, Shape, Gloss, Surface defects | Brown crust of bread, glossy chocolate |
| **Olfaction** | Nose | Aroma, Odor, Flavor notes (via retronasal) | Vanilla, garlic, fruity notes |
| **Gustation** | Tongue | Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, Umami (5 basic tastes) | Sugar, lemon, salt, quinine, MSG |
| **Tactile/Texture** | Skin, Mouth | Hardness, Crispness, Juiciness, Viscosity, Graininess | Crunchy chips, smooth yogurt |
| **Audition** | Ears | Sound during biting, pouring, sizzling | Snap of biscuit, fizz of soda |

> [!NOTE]
> **Flavor** is NOT a single sense — it is the **integrated perception** of taste (gustation), smell (olfaction), and chemesthesis (trigeminal nerve sensations like spiciness, coolness, pungency). This is why food loses "flavor" when you have a cold.

### Why Sensory Evaluation is Indispensable
> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Instruments cannot replicate human perception** — no machine can fully mimic the integrated experience of eating.
> - **Consumer acceptance** is ultimately determined by the human senses.
> - It is a **regulatory requirement** in many product development cycles.
> - It is the **only method** that measures consumer-liking, preference, and emotional response.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives and Functions of Food Quality & Safety Evaluation

The primary objectives are structured as follows:

- **Detection of Hazards**: Identification of physical (stones, metal), chemical (pesticides, toxins), and biological (pathogens, spoilage organisms) contaminants.
- **Quality Assurance (QA)**: Ensuring products meet established specifications at every stage of production.
- **Quality Control (QC)**: Operational techniques to fulfill quality requirements (in-process checks).
- **Shelf-Life Determination**: Establishing the time period during which the product remains safe and acceptable.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Adhering to national and international food laws.
- **Consumer Protection**: Preventing adulteration, misbranding, and fraud.
- **Process Optimization**: Using quality data to refine manufacturing parameters.

## 2.2 Classification of Food Evaluation Methods

```
Food Evaluation
├── Objective (Instrumental) Methods
│   ├── Physical (texture, color, viscosity)
│   ├── Chemical (proximate composition, pH, TBA)
│   └── Microbiological (TPC, coliform count, yeast & mold)
└── Subjective (Sensory) Methods
    ├── Discriminative Tests
    ├── Descriptive Tests
    └── Affective (Preference) Tests
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Neither objective nor subjective methods are superior in isolation.** The gold standard is **triangulation** — using both instrumental and sensory data together. For example, a colorimeter gives precise L*a*b* values, but only humans can confirm whether the color is *acceptable* to consumers.

## 2.3 Types of Sensory Tests — Comprehensive Classification

### A. Discriminative Tests (Difference Tests)
Used to determine if a **sensory difference exists** between two or more products. No preference is measured.

| Test Name | Purpose | Key Statistic |
|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test** | Detect any difference | Correct responses / Total |
| **Paired Comparison (Directional)** | Detect specific attribute difference | McNemar's test |
| **Duo-Trio Test** | Detect difference vs. reference | Number correct |
| **Two-Alternative Forced Choice (2-AFC)** | Forced choice between two | Signal Detection |
| **Ranking Test** | Order samples by intensity | Friedman test |

### B. Descriptive Tests
Used to **identify and quantify** specific sensory attributes of a product.

| Test Name | Description | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Flavor Profile** | Trained panel describes aroma & flavor | Descriptive terms + intensity |
| **Texture Profile** | Characterizes textural properties | Mechanical + geometric attributes |
| **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA)** | 10–12 trained panelists rate intensity on structured scales | Numerical intensity scores |
| **Spectrum Descriptive Analysis** | Uses universal intensity scales anchored with references | Absolute intensity values |
| **Free-Choice Profiling** | Panelists develop their own vocabulary | Individual vocabularies |

### C. Affective (Preference/Acceptance) Tests
Used to measure **consumer liking, preference, and acceptance** — the domain of marketing.

| Test Name | Sample Size | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Hedonic Rating (9-point)** | 75–150+ consumers | Liking score (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely) |
| **Paired Preference** | ≥50 consumers | % preferring each sample |
| **Acceptance Test** | Target consumers | Acceptance vs. rejection threshold |
| **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** | Target consumers | Penalty analysis for attribute optimization |

## 2.4 Sensory Attributes — The Four Pillars

### 1. Appearance
- **Color** (hue, saturation, brightness) — measured instrumentally as L*a*b*
- **Size, Shape, Symmetry**
- **Surface texture** (gloss, roughness)
- **Defects** (discoloration, bruises)

### 2. Texture
- **Mechanical properties**: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Viscosity, Elasticity, Adhesiveness
- **Geometric properties**: Granularity, Fibrousness, Crystallinity
- **Moisture properties**: Juiciness, Oiliness, Wetness
- **Primary texture parameters** (Szczesniak classification)

### 3. Flavor
- **Taste**: Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, Umami
- **Aroma/Orthonasal smell**: Detected through nostrils
- **Retronasal smell**: Detected through back of throat while eating
- **Chemesthesis**: Spicy (capsaicin), Cool (menthol), Pungent

### 4. Sound
- **Mastication sounds**: Crunch, crackle, crisp
- **Pouring sounds**: Fizz, splash
- **Correlates with freshness** and texture quality

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Triangle Test (correct responses)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Number correct}}{N}$ | Minimum correct for significance at α=0.05: with 30 panelists → **13 correct** |
| **Hedonic Mean Score** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum f \cdot x}{N}$ | Average consumer liking (1–9 scale) |
| **Water Activity (a_w)** | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Most bacteria grow above **0.91**; most molds above **0.70** |
| **pH Calculation** | $pH = -\log[H^{+}]$ | Acidic foods (pH < 4.6) inhibit *Clostridium botulinum* |
| **Standard Deviation (Sensory)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{N-1}}$ | Measures panelist consistency |
| **ANOVA for Sensory Data** | $F = \dfrac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}$ | Determines if samples differ significantly |
| **Tukey's HSD** | $HSD = q_{\alpha, k, df} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_{within}}{n}}$ | Post-hoc pairwise comparison |
| **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \cdot 100\%$ | Panelist repeatability: should be < **20%** |
| **Cronbach's Alpha** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\sum s_i^{2}}{s_t^{2}}\right)$ | Panel reliability: should be > **0.7** |
| **Beta-binomial (Triangle)** | $P_{max} = \dfrac{x + 0.5(N - x) + 0.5}{N + 1}$ | Confidence interval for p |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constants** (memorize these):
> - Triangle test significance (N=30, α=0.05): minimum correct = **13**
> - Minimum consumer panel size for affective tests: **75 panelists** (some standards require 100+)
> - Minimum trained panel size for descriptive analysis: **8–12 panelists**
> - Hedonic scale mid-point (5) represents **"neither like nor dislike"**
> - Sensory booths: **ISO 8589** standard; **red light** to mask color differences

## 2.6 Sensory Panel Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO Standards for Sensory Testing Environment** (ISO 8589):
> - Individual booths with controlled temperature (**20 ± 2°C**)
> - Neutral, odor-free environment
> - Standardized lighting (often **D65 daylight** or red for color-masking)
> - Sample preparation area separate from testing area
> - Positive air pressure to prevent odor infiltration
> - Sample presentation at controlled temperature (e.g., beverages at **15°C**, hot foods at **60°C**)

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

| Industry Application | Sensory Test Used | Business Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **New Product Development** | Descriptive + Affective | Finalize formula, launch decision |
| **Quality Control** | Discriminative (Triangle) | Release/reject batch |
| **Shelf-life studies** | Descriptive over time | Set expiry date |
| **Competitive benchmarking** | Affective + Descriptive | Reformulation to beat competitor |
| **Optimization** | JAR + Penalty Analysis | Adjust sugar/acid levels |
| **Sensory shelf-life** | Hedonic + Just-About-Right | Determine end of consumer acceptance |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Statistical Procedures in Sensory Evaluation

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Triangle Test Critical Number of Correct Responses

The triangle test presents three coded samples (two identical, one different) to each panelist. They must identify the odd sample. The null hypothesis is that panelists are **guessing** (probability of correct = 1/3).

**Step 1: State the null hypothesis**

$$H_0: p = \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{(no sensory difference; guessing)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the binomial distribution**

The probability that $x$ out of $N$ panelists guess correctly:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{N}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{N-x}$$

**Step 3: Compute critical value for one-tailed test at α = 0.05**

The critical number is the smallest $x$ such that the cumulative probability is ≤ 0.05.

For $N = 30$ panelists, using the binomial distribution with $p = 1/3$:

$$P(X \geq 13 \mid p = 1/3) = 0.0434$$

Since 0.0434 < 0.05, we reject $H_0$. So **13 or more correct out of 30** indicates a significant difference at the 5% level.

**Step 4: General rule-of-thumb (K-table)**

| Panelists (N) | Minimum correct for significance (α=0.05, one-tailed) |
|---|---|
| 20 | 10 |
| 30 | 13 |
| 40 | 16 |
| 50 | 19 |
| 60 | 22 |

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the 9-Point Hedonic Mean and Acceptance Threshold

The 9-point hedonic scale:
- 9 = Like extremely
- 8 = Like very much
- 7 = Like moderately
- 6 = Like slightly
- 5 = Neither like nor dislike
- 4 = Dislike slightly
- 3 = Dislike moderately
- 2 = Dislike very much
- 1 = Dislike extremely

**Step 1: Collect individual scores from N consumers.**

**Step 2: Compute weighted mean score**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}{N}$$

**Step 3: Apply acceptance criterion**

A product is considered **acceptable** if $\bar{X} \geq 6.0$ (between "like slightly" and "like moderately") for the target consumer group.

**Step 4: Compute percentage acceptance**

$$\% \text{Acceptance} = \frac{\text{Number of panelists scoring} \geq 6}{N} \times 100$$

A common commercial threshold: **≥ 75% of consumers must score ≥ 6** for market launch.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Two-Way ANOVA for Sensory Panelist × Sample

In descriptive analysis, we test whether samples differ AND whether panelists differ in their use of the scale.

**The linear model:**

$$x_{ij} = \mu + S_i + P_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where:
- $x_{ij}$ = score given by panelist $j$ to sample $i$
- $\mu$ = grand mean
- $S_i$ = effect of sample $i$ (fixed)
- $P_j$ = effect of panelist $j$ (random)
- $\epsilon_{ij}$ = random error, NID(0, $\sigma^2$)

**Hypotheses:**

$$H_0: S_1 = S_2 = \cdots = S_k = 0 \quad \text{(no sample effect)}$$
$$H_1: \text{at least one } S_i \neq 0$$

**Computation of Mean Squares:**

$$SS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_{ij} - \bar{\bar{x}})^{2}$$

$$SS_{samples} = n \sum_{i=1}^{k}(\bar{x}_{i \cdot} - \bar{\bar{x}})^{2}$$

$$SS_{panelists} = k \sum_{j=1}^{n}(\bar{x}_{\cdot j} - \bar{\bar{x}})^{2}$$

$$SS_{error} = SS_{total} - SS_{samples} - SS_{panelists}$$

**F-statistic for sample effect:**

$$F_{sample} = \frac{MS_{samples}}{MS_{error}} = \frac{SS_{samples} / (k-1)}{SS_{error} / [(k-1)(n-1)]}$$

**Decision rule:** If $F_{calculated} > F_{table}(\alpha, k-1, (k-1)(n-1))$, reject $H_0$ — the samples are significantly different.

### 3.1.4 Water Activity and Microbial Safety

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

Where $p$ = partial vapor pressure of water in food, $p_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water.

**Critical thresholds (memorize):**

$$a_w > 0.91 \Rightarrow \text{Pathogenic bacteria grow (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria)}$$
$$a_w > 0.86 \Rightarrow \text{Most spoilage bacteria grow}$$
$$a_w > 0.70 \Rightarrow \text{Most yeasts and molds grow}$$
$$a_w < 0.60 \Rightarrow \text{No microbial growth (xerophilic molds only)}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of the Right Sensory Test

| Research Question | Recommended Test | Panel Type | Sample Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Is there any difference between A and B?" | Triangle Test | Semi-trained | 25–40 |
| "Which is sweeter, A or B?" | Paired Comparison (directional) | Trained | 25–40 |
| "Describe the flavor profile of A" | QDA / Spectrum | Highly trained | 8–12 |
| "Do consumers like A or B more?" | Hedonic (9-pt) | Consumers | 75–150 |
| "Is A acceptable to the market?" | Acceptance Test | Target consumers | ≥ 100 |
| "How should I change the recipe?" | JAR + Penalty Analysis | Target consumers | 75–150 |
| "Rank these 4 samples by sourness" | Ranking | Semi-trained | 25–40 |
| "How does quality change over storage?" | Descriptive over time | Trained | 8–12 |
| "What is the shelf-life?" | Affective + Descriptive | Mixed | Variable |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Triangle Test

> **Problem**: A bakery has reformulated its bread and wants to know if a sensory difference exists compared to the original. 30 panelists are given a triangle test. Results: 14 panelists correctly identified the odd sample. Is there a significant difference at α = 0.05?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** State hypotheses.
$$H_0: p = \frac{1}{3} \quad H_1: p > \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2:** Critical value from K-table for N=30, α=0.05: **13 correct responses**.

**Step 3:** Observed correct responses = **14**.

**Step 4:** Since 14 > 13, reject $H_0$.

**Step 5:** Exact probability:

$$P(X \geq 14 \mid N = 30, p = 1/3) = 0.0233$$

Since $0.0233 < 0.05$, the difference is **statistically significant** at the 5% level.

**Step 6:** Conclusion. There is sufficient evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that a **sensory difference exists** between the reformulated and original bread. The reformulation has produced a perceivable change.

**Step 7:** Practical recommendation. The reformulation should be evaluated for **acceptance** via an affective test (e.g., hedonic) before launch — significance ≠ preference.

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the classification of sensory evaluation tests with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure and statistical analysis of the Triangle test used in sensory evaluation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Sensory Evaluation Tests (7 marks)**

Sensory evaluation tests are broadly classified into **three major categories** based on the type of question being answered and the type of panel used:

**1. Discriminative (Difference) Tests — (3 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Determine if a perceptible difference exists between two or more products. No preference is assessed.
- **Panel type:** Semi-trained, 25–40 panelists, screened for sensory acuity.
- **Examples:**
  - *Triangle Test:* Three samples (two identical, one different); panelist identifies the odd one. Used in QC to detect process changes.
  - *Paired Comparison Test:* Two samples; panelist selects which has more of a specified attribute. Used to verify reformulation.
  - *Duo-Trio Test:* Three samples with a reference; panelist identifies which matches the reference. Used for sensory aging studies.
  - *Two-Alternative Forced Choice (2-AFC):* Two samples; forced choice of which has more of an attribute. Highly sensitive.

**2. Descriptive Tests — (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Identify and quantify the intensity of specific sensory attributes (appearance, aroma, flavor, texture).
- **Panel type:** Highly trained, 8–12 panelists, with 60–100+ hours of training, validated for repeatability and panel agreement.
- **Examples:**
  - *Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA):* Panelists develop vocabulary, rate intensities on 15-cm line scales.
  - *Flavor Profile Method:* Developed by Arthur D. Little; group discussion-based profiling.
  - *Texture Profile Method:* Based on Szczesniak's classification of textural parameters.
  - *Spectrum Descriptive Analysis:* Uses anchored reference standards for absolute intensity.

**3. Affective (Preference/Acceptance) Tests — (2 marks)**
- **Purpose:** Measure consumer liking, preference, and acceptance.
- **Panel type:** Untrained consumers, 75–150+ panelists, representative of the target market.
- **Examples:**
  - *9-Point Hedonic Scale:* Most widely used; measures degree of liking.
  - *Paired Preference Test:* Forced choice of preferred sample.
  - *Acceptance Test:* Compares against a "just acceptable" threshold.
  - *Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale:* 5- or 7-point scale with "just about right" midpoint; penalty analysis reveals optimization targets.

**(b) Triangle Test — Procedure and Statistical Analysis (7 marks)**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Sample preparation:** Three coded samples (e.g., 3-digit random numbers) are presented — two identical (A, A) and one different (B). Two orderings are used: ABB and BAA to balance position bias.
2. **Panel selection:** 25–40 semi-trained panelists, screened for sensory acuity, no allergies, no consumption of strong flavors 1 hour prior.
3. **Test conditions:** Conducted in ISO 8589 sensory booths, controlled temperature, neutral lighting (often red to mask color), 20°C.
4. **Instructions:** "Taste each sample in the order presented, from left to right. Identify the ODD sample. You must choose — guessing is required."
5. **Rinse protocol:** Water and unsalted crackers provided between samples to cleanse the palate.
6. **Data collection:** Each panelist's choice is recorded. Number of correct responses is tallied.

**Statistical Analysis (3 marks):**

The null hypothesis $H_0: p = 1/3$ (panelists are guessing) is tested against $H_1: p > 1/3$ using the binomial distribution:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{N}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{N-x}$$

With $N = 30$ panelists and $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), the critical value is **13 correct responses** (from standard K-table).

If the observed number of correct responses ≥ 13, $H_0$ is rejected — a significant difference exists.

**Strengths:** Simple, sensitive, well-suited to QC.
**Limitations:** Does not identify the attribute causing the difference; forces guessing (some panelists may avoid guessing → use 2-AFC or forced-choice variants).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the procedure for conducting a 9-point Hedonic rating test. What are its advantages and limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the roles of trained and consumer panels in sensory evaluation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 9-Point Hedonic Rating Test (7 marks)**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Define the test objective:** Identify target consumer demographic, sample size (minimum 75), and product comparison goals.
2. **Sample preparation:** Randomly code samples with 3-digit numbers, prepare at consistent temperature (e.g., 15°C for beverages), identical portion sizes (~30 mL for liquids, ~10 g for solids).
3. **Panel recruitment:** Recruit 75–150 consumers matching the target demographic (age, gender, usage frequency, geography). Screen for food allergies and willingness to consume the product category.
4. **Test environment:** Sensory booths or a centralized consumer testing facility (central location test, CLT) with controlled conditions.
5. **Sample presentation:** Serve samples in a randomized order across panelists (to balance order and carry-over effects). Use Williams Latin Square or similar design.
6. **Rating scale explanation:** Each panelist is given the 9-point hedonic scale with verbal anchors (1 = dislike extremely, 5 = neither like nor dislike, 9 = like extremely). Written instructions are also provided.
7. **Scoring:** Each consumer rates each sample for overall liking. Optional sub-attributes (aroma, texture, flavor) may also be rated.
8. **Data analysis:** Calculate the mean hedonic score, frequency distribution, and percentage acceptance (score ≥ 6). Use ANOVA and post-hoc tests to compare samples.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Widely accepted** in industry and academia; easy to understand by consumers.
- **Generates both numerical and percentage data** for decision-making.
- **Sensitive to small differences** in liking.
- **No trained panel required** — uses real consumers.
- **Provides data for statistical analysis** (ANOVA, mean comparison, clustering).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Forced scale use:** Some consumers use only the middle range.
- **Cultural bias:** Scale interpretation varies across cultures.
- **Doesn't tell *why* a sample is liked/disliked** — must be paired with descriptive or JAR analysis.
- **Hedonic score ≠ purchase intent** — additional intent questions needed.

**(b) Roles of Trained and Consumer Panels (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Trained Panel | Consumer Panel |
|---|---|---|
| **Panelists** | Screened, trained for 60–100+ hours | Untrained, representative of target market |
| **Number** | 8–12 panelists | 75–150+ panelists |
| **Function** | **Detect, describe, quantify** sensory attributes | **Measure liking, preference, acceptance** |
| **Output** | Intensity scores, descriptive terms | Hedonic scores, preference percentages |
| **Test type** | Descriptive (QDA, Spectrum, Profile) | Affective (Hedonic, Preference) |
| **Cost** | Lower per session (fewer panelists) | Higher overall (large sample size) |
| **Sensory acuity** | Validated, repeatable | Variable, real-world |

**Trained Panel — Examples (3 marks):**
- *Example 1:* A dairy company wants to optimize the *creamy mouthfeel* of its yogurt. A trained panel performs **Texture Profile Analysis** to objectively measure thickness, smoothness, and spoonability across multiple formulations.
- *Example 2:* A flavor house develops a new citrus flavor. A trained panel uses **QDA** to rate citrus character, sweetness, bitterness, and aromatic notes on a 15-cm line scale, guiding the flavorist in fine-tuning.
- *Example 3:* Quality control of a beverage. A trained panel conducts a **triangle test** on production batches to detect any process-induced changes.

**Consumer Panel — Examples (3 marks):**
- *Example 1:* Before launching a new snack, the company runs a **central location test (CLT)** with 150 consumers using the **9-point hedonic scale** to assess overall liking.
- *Example 2:* A bakery chain uses an **acceptance test** to confirm that the reformulated low-sugar cake meets consumer standards (≥ 75% scoring 6+).
- *Example 3:* A pet food manufacturer uses a **JAR scale** with 100 dog owners to identify whether the aroma is "just about right," guiding product reformulation via penalty analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating significance with preference:** A significant difference (e.g., triangle test) does **NOT** mean consumers prefer one product. Always follow up with an affective test.
> 2. **Wrong panel type for wrong question:** Using a consumer panel for descriptive analysis (or vice versa) is a common, costly error.
> 3. **Ignoring carry-over effects:** In within-subject designs, residual flavor from the first sample biases the second. Always use palate cleansers and randomized order.
> 4. **Forgetting to control sensory biases:** Sample position (left vs. right), order (first vs. last), and lighting (red for color-masking) must be controlled.
> 5. **Misinterpreting pH and a_w thresholds:** Confusing "pathogen growth limit" with "spoilage limit." Pathogens stop at a_w ≈ 0.86, but *Staphylococcus aureus* tolerates a_w as low as 0.83 in high-salt environments.
> 6. **Omitting sample size justification:** Always justify panel size — for affective tests ≥ 75, for descriptive 8–12, for discriminative 25–40.
> 7. **Mixing up "flavor" with "taste":** Flavor = taste + smell + chemesthesis. Confusing these costs marks in definitions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme**

**1. Core Definitions (must memorize):**
- Food Quality vs. Food Safety — quality is *desirability*; safety is *harmlessness*
- Sensory Evaluation — scientific measurement of human responses to food
- Flavor = Taste + Olfaction + Chemesthesis (NOT a single sense)
- Water activity ($a_w$) — partial pressure ratio; dimensionless (0.0 to 1.0)
- HACCP — Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point

**2. Five Senses in Sensory Evaluation:**
- Vision, Olfaction, Gustation, Tactile, Audition
- Five basic tastes: Sweet, Sour, Salty, Bitter, **Umami**

**3. Three Classes of Sensory Tests:**
- **Discriminative** — difference? (Triangle, 2-AFC, Duo-Trio)
- **Descriptive** — what & how much? (QDA, Spectrum, Profile)
- **Affective** — do you like it? (Hedonic, Preference, JAR)

**4. Critical Numbers to Memorize:**
- Triangle test, N=30, α=0.05 → **13 correct responses**
- Consumer panel minimum → **75 panelists** (industry standard: 100+)
- Trained panel size → **8–12 panelists**
- Hedonic acceptance threshold → **score ≥ 6** (≥ 75% of consumers)
- Pathogen growth → $a_w > 0.91$
- *C. botulinum* inhibition → **pH < 4.6**
- Sensory booth temperature → **20 ± 2°C**
- ISO 8589 governs sensory booth design

**5. Standards & Regulations (must know):**
- **FSSAI** (India), **FDA** (USA), **EFSA** (Europe), **Codex Alimentarius** (International)
- **ISO 8589** — Sensory analysis booth design
- **ISO 5492** — Sensory analysis vocabulary
- **AOAC International** — Analytical methods
- **Big-8 Allergens** — milk, egg, fish, shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans

**6. Key Statistical Tests:**
- Triangle test → **Binomial distribution**
- Hedonic → **Mean, ANOVA, % acceptance**
- Descriptive → **Two-way ANOVA (sample × panelist)**
- Panel reliability → **Cronbach's α ≥ 0.7**
- Panelist repeatability → **CV < 20%**
- Post-hoc → **Tukey's HSD, Duncan's MRT**

**7. Critical Thresholds for Food Safety:**
- pH < 4.6 → inhibits *Clostridium botulinum* (acidic foods)
- $a_w < 0.85$ → inhibits most pathogens
- TPC acceptance limit (general) → < **$10^5$ CFU/g**
- Yeast & mold → < **$10^2$ – $10^3$ CFU/g** depending on food
- Coliform → **absent in 0.1 g** for ready-to-eat foods

**8. Practical Tips for KTU Exam:**
- Always draw a **comparison table** when differentiating methods.
- For numerical problems, **state hypotheses first, then compute, then conclude.**
- Cite **ISO/FSSAI standards** wherever applicable — adds marks.
- Use **mermaid diagrams or labeled flowcharts** to score well in 'discuss' type questions.
- For 14-mark questions, divide answer as: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks** with sub-headings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumental Measurement of Sensory Attributes: Colour, Viscosity, Texture, Specific Gravity, Rheological and Textural Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT746 - EMERGING TECHNIQUES IN FOOD QUALITY & SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Instrumental Measurement of Sensory Attributes: Colour, Viscosity, Texture, Specific Gravity, Rheological and Textural Characteristics ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Instrumental Measurement of Sensory Attributes — Foundational Definition In food quality and safety engineering, **sensory attributes** are the perceived characteristics of a food product detected through human senses (sight, touch, taste, smell, hearing). However, human sensory panels suffer from subjectivity, fatigue, cost, and inconsistency. **Instrumental measurement** replaces or complements the human panel by providing **objective, reproducible, quantitative** data using calibrated physical and chemical instruments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (High-Yield):** > Instrumental measurement of sensory attributes refers to the use of standardized physical instruments (colorimeters, viscometers, texture analyzers, pycnometers, rheometers) to quantify the perceived sensory properties of food — namely **Colour, Viscosity, Texture, Specific Gravity**, and their **Rheological & Textural characteristics** — in alignment with objective engineering units rather than subjective human descriptors. The five pillars covered in this module are: | Sensory Attribute | Human Sense | Instrument Family | |---|---|---| | Colour | Sight (Vision) | Colorimeter / Spectrophotometer | | Viscosity | Touch (Mouthfeel) | Viscometer / Rheometer | | Texture | Touch (Finger/Mouth) | Texture Analyzer (TPA) | | Specific Gravity | Kinesthesia (Weight perception) | Pycnometer / Hydrometer / Digital Density Meter | | Rheology/Textural characteristics | Touch / Mouthfeel | Rotational Rheometer, Texture Profiler | --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogies for Each Attribute > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu Inspector"** > Imagine a food critic visiting a restaurant kitchen with a clipboard. Instead of *tasting* every dish and writing vague adjectives ("a bit thick," "nicely orange"), the inspector brings **measuring tools**: a ruler for colour intensity, a stopwatch for pour-thickness, a spring-gauge for chewiness, a weighing balance for density, and a flow-tube for pour-behavior. That is exactly what instrumental measurement does — it **translates subjective perception into engineering numbers**. - **Colour** → Like comparing paint swatches under standardized daylight (D65 illuminant) rather than guessing. - **Viscosity** → Like measuring how fast honey pours versus water through a funnel. - **Texture** → Like squeezing a stress ball and measuring how it rebounds. - **Specific Gravity** → Like floating an egg — sinking in plain water but floating in salt water tells us its relative density. - **Rheology** → Like comparing ketchup (shake → flows) vs. yogurt (needs spoon) — both are "thick" but flow differently under stress. --- ### 1.3 Why Instrumental Measurement? — The KTU Justification > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU mandates this topic because of the increasing industrial reliance on **Process Analytical Technology (PAT)**, **ISO 17025 lab accreditation**, and **HACCP/ISO 22000** quality systems — all of which demand traceable, calibrated numerical data, NOT panelist impressions. Key engineering standards referenced globally: - **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** — colour measurement - **ASTM D2196 / ISO 2555** — rotational viscosity - **AOAC Official Methods** — specific gravity of foods - **Bourne's TPA (1978)** — texture profile analysis ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 COLOUR — Instrumental Theory Colour perception involves three components: 1. **Light source** (illuminant — typically **D65** for daylight simulation) 2. **Object** (reflectance/transmittance properties) 3. **Observer** (standard observer with defined cone response — **CIE 1931 2°** or **1964 10°**) #### 2.1.1 CIE L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB) Color Space The most widely used uniform colour space in food analysis. $$L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$$ $$a^* = 500 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{X}{X_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) \right]$$ $$b^* = 200 \cdot \left[ f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - f\!\left(\frac{Z}{Z_n}\right) \right]$$ Where the function $f(t)$ is defined as: $$f(t) = \begin{cases} t^{1/3} & \text{if } t > \left(\frac{6}{29}\right)^3 \\[6pt] \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{29}{6}\right)^2 t + \frac{4}{29} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ - $L^*$ = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white) - $a^*$ = Red–Green axis (+ve = red, –ve = green) - $b^*$ = Yellow–Blue axis (+ve = yellow, –ve = blue) #### 2.1.2 Total Colour Difference (ΔE\*ab) $$\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(L^*_2 - L^*_1)^2 + (a^*_2 - a^*_1)^2 + (b^*_2 - b^*_1)^2}$$ #### 2.1.3 Hue Angle (h°) $$h^\circ = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{b^*}{a^*}\right)$$ #### 2.1.4 Chroma (C\*) $$C^* = \sqrt{a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$$ #### 2.1.5 Whiteness Index (WI) — For Dairy/Flour $$WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100 - L^*)^2 + a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$$ #### 2.1.6 Browning Index (BI) — For Bakery/Meat $$BI = \frac{100 \cdot (x - 0.31)}{0.172}$$ Where $x = \dfrac{a^* + 1.75 L^*}{5.645 L^* + a^* - 3.012 b^*}$ --- ### 2.2 VISCOSITY — Instrumental Theory **Viscosity ($\eta$)** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow, defined by Newton's law of viscosity: $$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa) - $\eta$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) - $\dot{\gamma}$ = Shear rate (s⁻¹) #### 2.2.1 Kinematic Viscosity $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$ Units: **m²/s** (SI) or **centistokes (cSt)** in practice. #### 2.2.2 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids The constitutive equation generalizes to: $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ - **Newtonian**: $n = 1$ (water, milk, oils, honey at moderate shear) - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**: $n < 1$ (ketchup, yogurt, blood) - **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**: $n > 1$ (cornstarch suspension, wet sand) - **Bingham plastic**: Requires yield stress $\tau_0$ before flow ($\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_p \dot{\gamma}$) - **Casson fluid**: $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$ (chocolate, blood) #### 2.2.3 Apparent Viscosity $$\eta_{apparent} = \frac{\tau_{applied}}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ #### 2.2.4 Capillary Viscometer — Hagen-Poiseuille Law $$\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \cdot t}{8 L V}$$ Used in **Ostwald** and **Ubbelohde** viscometers. --- ### 2.3 TEXTURE — Instrumental Theory #### 2.3.1 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) — Bourne (1978) The TPA double-compression test mimics the two-bite mastication cycle and yields **seven primary parameters**: | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Hardness | $H$ | Peak force on first compression | N (or g) | | Cohesiveness | $C$ | Ratio of positive force areas (A2/A1) | Dimensionless | | Springiness | $S$ | Distance recovered after first compression | mm | | Adhesiveness | $A$ | Negative force area during first bite | N·s | | Gumminess | $G$ | $H \times C$ | N | | Chewiness | $Ch$ | $G \times S$ | N·mm | | Resilience | $R$ | Ratio of up-stroke to down-stroke areas (first cycle) | Dimensionless | Key TPA formulas: $$Gumminess = Hardness \times Cohesiveness$$ $$Chewiness = Gumminess \times Springiness = H \times C \times S$$ #### 2.3.2 Three-Point Bend Test (for brittle foods) $$\sigma = \frac{3FL}{2bh^2}$$ $$\epsilon = \frac{6D\delta}{L^2}$$ $$E = \frac{FL^3}{4bh^3\delta}$$ Where: - $F$ = Load at break - $L$ = Support span - $b$ = Sample width - $h$ = Sample thickness - $\delta$ = Deflection - $D$ = Diameter #### 2.3.3 Shear Test (Kramer Shear Cell) Force to shear through multiple food pieces — used for vegetables and meats. --- ### 2.4 SPECIFIC GRAVITY — Instrumental Theory **Specific gravity (SG)** is the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of a reference substance (water at 4 °C for liquids, air for gases). $$SG = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{reference}}$$ #### 2.4.1 Pycnometer Method $$SG = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{(m_3 - m_1) - (m_4 - m_2)}$$ Where: - $m_1$ = Empty pycnometer mass - $m_2$ = Pycnometer + sample mass - $m_3$ = Pycnometer + water mass - $m_4$ = Pycnometer + sample + water mass #### 2.4.2 Digital Density Meter (Oscillating U-Tube) $$\rho = K \cdot \tau^2 - C$$ Where $\tau$ is the oscillation period and $K$, $C$ are instrument constants calibrated with air and water. #### 2.4.3 Brix Conversion (°Brix ≈ % sucrose by weight) $$^\circ \text{Brix} \approx \text{SG} \times (0.0042 \cdot \text{SG} - 0.0034) + 0.9996$$ Or more commonly for juices: $$^\circ \text{Brix} = 261.529 \cdot \frac{SG - 1}{SG} + 0.999$$ --- ### 2.5 RHEOLOGICAL CHARACTERISTICS — Theory #### 2.5.1 Viscoelastic Models | Model | Constitutive Equation | Behavior | |---|---|---| | Maxwell | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\dot{\tau}}{G} + \dfrac{\tau}{\eta}$ | Liquid-like long-term | | Kelvin-Voigt | $\tau = G \gamma + \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Solid-like creep | | Burger's | Series Maxwell ‖ Kelvin-Voigt | Full creep + recovery | #### 2.5.2 Dynamic (Oscillatory) Rheology When sinusoidal strain $\gamma(t) = \gamma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied, the stress response is: $$\tau(t) = \tau_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta)$$ - **Storage modulus** $G' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \cos \delta$ (elastic, solid-like) - **Loss modulus** $G'' = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \sin \delta$ (viscous, liquid-like) - **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta = \dfrac{G''}{G'}$ - **Complex modulus** $G^* = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$ **Phase angle interpretation:** - $\delta \to 0°$ → Elastic solid (gel) - $\delta \to 90°$ → Viscous liquid - $G' = G''$ → Gel point / crossover --- ### 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Quick Revision Table) | # | Parameter | Formula | SI Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Shear stress | $\tau = F/A$ | Pa | | 2 | Shear rate | $\dot{\gamma} = v/h$ | s⁻¹ | | 3 | Newton's law | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | | 4 | Power-law fluid | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | Pa | | 5 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \eta/\rho$ | m²/s | | 6 | Hagen-Poiseuille | $\eta = \pi R^4 \Delta P t / (8LV)$ | Pa·s | | 7 | CIELAB L* | $L^* = 116 f(Y/Y_n) - 16$ | – | | 8 | ΔE\*ab | $\sqrt{(\Delta L)^2+(\Delta a)^2+(\Delta b)^2}$ | – | | 9 | Hue angle | $h^\circ = \tan^{-1}(b^*/a^*)$ | degrees | | 10 | Specific gravity | $SG = \rho_{sub}/\rho_{water}$ | dimensionless | | 11 | Pycnometer SG | $(m_2-m_1)/[(m_3-m_1)-(m_4-m_2)]$ | – | | 12 | TPA Gumminess | $H \times C$ | N | | 13 | TPA Chewiness | $H \times C \times S$ | N·mm | | 14 | Storage modulus | $G' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\cos\delta$ | Pa | | 15 | Loss modulus | $G'' = (\tau_0/\gamma_0)\sin\delta$ | Pa | | 16 | Loss tangent | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | – | | 17 | Casson model | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_c \dot{\gamma}}$ | – | | 18 | Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{app} = \tau/\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | --- ### 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Dairy Industry**: $L^*$ value predicts butter whiteness; $a^*$ monitors oxidation (pink defect). - **Edible Oils**: Viscosity index (VI) classifies lubricant performance. - **Bakery**: TPA chewiness correlates with staling; $G'$/$G''$ crossover predicts bread freshness. - **Juice Industry**: °Brix (refractometer) directly tied to sugar content and pricing. - **Chocolate Manufacture**: Casson plastic viscosity + yield stress is a quality passport. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen-Poiseuille Law for Capillary Viscometry We start with the **Navier-Stokes equation** for steady, laminar, incompressible, fully-developed flow through a cylindrical tube of radius $R$: $$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ **Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate once: $$r \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} r^2 + C_1$$ **Step 2:** Apply the no-slip boundary condition: at $r = R$, $v = 0$. First, divide by $r$: $$\frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$ For the velocity to remain finite at $r = 0$, we must have $C_1 = 0$. Integrate again: $$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$ **Step 3:** This is the parabolic **Poiseuille velocity profile**. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is found by integrating $v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ over the cross-section: $$Q = \int_0^R v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$ **Step 4:** Since $Q = V/t$ (volume $V$ discharged in time $t$), solving for $\eta$ gives the working equation of any capillary viscometer: $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P \cdot t}{8 L V}}$$ **Conversion to kinematic viscosity** divides by density: $$\nu = \frac{\eta}{\rho}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage and Loss Modulus from Complex Compliance Start with complex notation. Apply a complex strain $\gamma^*(t) = \gamma_0 e^{i\omega t}$, generating a complex stress $\tau^*(t) = \tau_0 e^{i(\omega t + \delta)}$. **Step 1:** Define the complex modulus: $$G^* = \frac{\tau^*(t)}{\gamma^*(t)} = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} e^{i\delta}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Euler's identity $e^{i\delta} = \cos\delta + i \sin\delta$: $$G^* = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \cos\delta + i \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0} \sin\delta$$ **Step 3:** Identify real and imaginary parts: $$G' = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0}\cos\delta \quad ; \quad G'' = \frac{\tau_0}{\gamma_0}\sin\delta$$ **Step 4:** Magnitude (the "stiffness" at that frequency): $$|G^*| = \sqrt{G'^2 + G''^2}$$ **Step 5:** Phase angle (the ratio of viscous to elastic response): $$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CIELAB L\* from Tristimulus Y The CIE L* formula was derived to be **perceptually uniform** — equal numerical differences in L\* correspond to equal perceived lightness differences. Starting from the cube-root nonlinearity intended to linearize the Munsell Value scale: **Step 1:** Define a normalized stimulus function: $$f(t) = t^{1/3} \quad \text{for } t > \left(\tfrac{6}{29}\right)^3$$ **Step 2:** For small $t$, a linear tangent is matched at the breakpoint to ensure continuity of the first derivative: $$f(t) = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{29}{6}\right)^2 t + \frac{4}{29} \quad \text{for } t \leq \left(\tfrac{6}{29}\right)^3$$ **Step 3:** The lightness L* is then defined so that: - $Y/Y_n = 1$ (perfect white) → $L^* = 100$ - $Y/Y_n = 0$ (perfect black) → $L^* = 0$ $$L^* = 116 \cdot f\!\left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right) - 16$$ **Step 4:** Verify the black point. At $t = 0$: $f(0) = 4/29$, so $L^* = 116 \cdot (4/29) - 16 = 16 - 16 = 0$. ✓ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A capillary viscometer has $R = 0.5 \text{ mm}$, $L = 10 \text{ cm}$. A honey sample flows through under $\Delta P = 200 \text{ Pa}$ and takes 45 s to discharge $V = 5 \text{ mL}$. Given the density of honey is $\rho = 1420 \text{ kg/m}^3$, calculate the dynamic and kinematic viscosity. **Step 1 — Unit conversion:** $$R = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$ $$V = 5 \text{ mL} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Hagen-Poiseuille:** $$\eta = \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 \cdot 200 \cdot 45}{8 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \cdot 9000}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\eta = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.42 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s} \approx 0.442 \text{ mPa·s}$$ Wait — let me recompute carefully: $$R^4 = (5 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 625 \times 10^{-16} = 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \text{ m}^4$$ $$\pi R^4 \Delta P t = 3.14159 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 200 \times 45 = 1.767 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$8LV = 8 \times 0.10 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\eta = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.42 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 3 — Kinematic viscosity:** $$\nu = \frac{4.42 \times 10^{-4}}{1420} = 3.11 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 0.311 \text{ cSt}$$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Instrument | Sample Type | Recommended Colour Instrument | Recommended Viscosity Tool | Texture Tool | SG Tool | |---|---|---|---|---| | Milk, dairy | Tristimulus colorimeter | Rotational (cone-plate) | Penetrometer | Lactometer (hydrometer) | | Fruit juice | Spectrophotometer | Ostwald capillary | – | Refractometer (°Brix) | | Honey | Hunter Lab | Falling-ball (Höppler) | Penetrometer | Pycnometer | | Chocolate melt | – | Casson-fit rotational | – | – | | Bread / cake | L\*a\*b\* colorimeter | – | TPA + 3-point bend | Volume displacement | | Meat / fish | Hunter L\*a\*b\* | – | Warner-Bratzler shear | – | | Sauces, ketchup | – | Brookfield rotational | Back-extrusion | Density cup | | Powdered spices | Munsell / spectrophotometer | – | – | Tapped density | | Yogurt | – | Bob/cup rotational | Back-extrusion | Pycnometer | | Edible oil | Lovibond / tintometer | Ostwald (kinematic) | – | Digital density meter | --- ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Tree for Viscosity ``` START │ ├── Liquid (flows without threshold)? │ YES → Newtonian or non-Newtonian? │ │ │ │ │ ├── Newtonian (water, oil, milk) → Capillary OR rotational │ │ └── Non-Newtonian (ketchup, paste) → Rotational, sweep shear rate │ NO → Solid-like / gel? │ │ │ ├── Weak gel (jelly, pudding) → Oscillatory, find G' = G'' crossover │ └── Strong gel / dough → Creep test, find J(t) compliance END ``` ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture — Complete Instrumental Sensory Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Food Sample] --> B[Standardized Sample Preparation
temp, geometry, equilibration] B --> C1[Colour Lab] B --> C2[Viscosity Lab] B --> C3[Texture Lab] B --> C4[Density / SG Lab] B --> C5[Rheology Lab] C1 --> D1[Colorimeter
L a b] C1 --> D2[Spectrophotometer
reflectance] C2 --> E1[Capillary Viscometer
Ostwald, Ubbelohde] C2 --> E2[Rotational Viscometer
Brookfield] C2 --> E3[Falling Ball
Höppler] C3 --> F1[Texture Analyzer
TPA double bite] C3 --> F2[Kramer Shear Cell] C3 --> F3[Three Point Bend] C4 --> G1[Pycnometer] C4 --> G2[Hydrometer] C4 --> G3[Digital Density Meter
Oscillating U tube] C4 --> G4[Refractometer
Brix] C5 --> H1[Cone Plate Rheometer
flow curve] C5 --> H2[Oscillatory Rheometer
G prime, G double prime] D1 --> Z[Statistical Analysis
and Quality Report] D2 --> Z E1 --> Z E2 --> Z E3 --> Z F1 --> Z F2 --> Z F3 --> Z G1 --> Z G2 --> Z G3 --> Z G4 --> Z H1 --> Z H2 --> Z Z --> Z1[CO1 Validation] Z --> Z2[HACCP / ISO 17025
Compliance Report] Z --> Z3[SPC Charts
Control Limits] style A fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style Z fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style Z1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 style Z2 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 style Z3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 style B fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 style C2 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 style C3 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 style C4 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 style C5 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA) Force–Time Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Probe Contact] -->|Down stroke| B[Hardness Peak H] B --> C[Compression Phase A1] C --> D[Adhesiveness Negative Loop] D --> E[Withdrawal Upstroke] E -->|Retract pause| F[Wait Second Bite] F --> G[Probe Re engages] G -->|Down stroke 2| H[Second Compression A2] H --> I[Springiness measured as recovery distance] I --> J[Final Withdraw] B -.Peak.-> K[Hardness H] C -.Area.-> L[Area A1] H -.Peak 2.-> M[Cohesiveness equals A2 divided by A1] D -.Area.-> N[Adhesiveness] I -.Distance.-> O[Springiness S] K --> P[TPA Outputs] L --> P M --> P N --> P O --> P P --> Q[Gumminess equals H times C] P --> R[Chewiness equals Gumminess times Springiness] P --> S[Resilience equals upstroke area ratio] style A [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Define CIELAB colour space. State the significance of L\*, a\*, b\* values in food quality analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** CIELAB (CIE 1976 L\*a\*b\*) is a perceptually uniform, device-independent colour space developed by the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage. It expresses any colour using three coordinates: 1. **L\*** — Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white). In food analysis, it measures whiteness of milk, flour, rice, or lightness of meat. 2. **a\*** — Red–green axis (+ve red, –ve green). Used to monitor meat browning, tomato redness, and chlorophyll loss in vegetables. 3. **b\*** — Yellow–blue axis (+ve yellow, –ve blue). Tracks yellowing of oils, ripening of fruits, and Maillard browning. Significance: CIELAB allows **objective numerical comparison** of food colour independent of lighting/viewer, enabling quality control, batch-to-batch consistency, and shelf-life studies. The ΔE\*ab quantifies perceptible colour change. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Differentiate between Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids with two food examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Newtonian | Non-Newtonian | |---|---|---| | Viscosity behaviour | Constant with shear rate | Varies with shear rate | | Constitutive equation | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ or similar | | Flow index | $n = 1$ | $n \neq 1$ | | Examples | Water, milk, refined oils, air | Ketchup, yogurt, blood, chocolate, cornstarch slurry | | Measurement | Single viscosity value sufficient | Flow curve required | **Newtonian food examples:** (i) Water (η ≈ 1 mPa·s at 20 °C), (ii) Refined sunflower oil (η ≈ 50 mPa·s). **Non-Newtonian food examples:** (i) Ketchup (shear-thinning pseudoplastic), (ii) Cornstarch suspension in water (shear-thickening dilatant). --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, components, and working of a tristimulus colorimeter** for food colour measurement. Describe the CIELAB colour space with all three equations and state any **four derived parameters** used in food analysis. (7 marks) **(b)** A juice sample has $L^* = 42.3$, $a^* = 18.5$, $b^* = 35.2$. A reference standard has $L^* = 45.0$, $a^* = 16.0$, $b^* = 32.0$. Compute the **total colour difference (ΔE\*ab)**, the **chroma (C\*)**, and the **hue angle (h°)**. Comment on whether the difference is visually perceptible. (7 marks) --- ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Principle (2 marks):** A tristimulus colorimeter measures colour by illuminating the sample with a standardized light source (typically a pulsed xenon arc simulating D65 daylight), collecting the reflected light through **three silicon photodiodes** filtered to mimic the CIE standard observer's X (red), Y (green), and Z (blue) response curves. **Components (2 marks):** 1. **Illumination system** — Tungsten-halogen or xenon lamp + D65 filter. 2. **Integrating sphere** — Coats the inside with BaSO₄ to provide uniform diffuse illumination. 3. **Sample port** — Glass slide or cup holder for liquid/solid samples. 4. **Detector assembly** — Three filtered photodiodes (X, Y, Z) or a single diode with rotating filter wheel. 5. **Microprocessor** — Converts raw signals to X, Y, Z tristimulus values then to L\*a\*b\*. 6. **Display** — LCD/LED showing numerical values. **Working (1 mark):** The sample is placed at the port. Light reflects off the sample, is captured by the integrating sphere, filtered into X/Y/Z components, and the microprocessor applies the CIELAB transformation equations to output L\*, a\*, b\*. **CIELAB Equations (1 mark):** $$L^* = 116 \cdot f(Y/Y_n) - 16, \quad a^* = 500[f(X/X_n) - f(Y/Y_n)], \quad b^* = 200[f(Y/Y_n) - f(Z/Z_n)]$$ where $f(t) = t^{1/3}$ if $t > (6/29)^3$, else linear branch. **Four derived parameters (1 mark):** 1. **ΔE\*ab** — total colour difference. 2. **C\*** — chroma (saturation). 3. **h°** — hue angle. 4. **WI** — Whiteness Index (dairy/flour) or **BI** — Browning Index (bakery/meat). --- ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Compute ΔL\*, Δa\*, Δb\*:** $$\Delta L^* = 42.3 - 45.0 = -2.7$$ $$\Delta a^* = 18.5 - 16.0 = +2.5$$ $$\Delta b^* = 35.2 - 32.0 = +3.2$$ **Step 2 — Apply the ΔE\*ab formula:** $$\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(-2.7)^2 + (2.5)^2 + (3.2)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{7.29 + 6.25 + 10.24} = \sqrt{23.78}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta E^*_{ab} \approx 4.88}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Chroma C\* (use sample values):** $$C^* = \sqrt{(18.5)^2 + (35.2)^2} = \sqrt{342.25 + 1239.04} = \sqrt{1581.29}$$ $$\boxed{C^* \approx 39.77}$$ **Step 4 — Compute hue angle h°:** $$h^\circ = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{35.2}{18.5}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.9027)$$ $$\boxed{h^\circ \approx 62.27^\circ}$$ **Step 5 — Perceptibility comment:** According to standard perceptibility thresholds: - ΔE < 1 → not perceptible - 1 ≤ ΔE < 2 → barely perceptible - 2 ≤ ΔE < 3.5 → noticeable on close inspection - ΔE > 3.5 → clearly perceptible difference - ΔE > 5 → strong difference Since **ΔE\*ab ≈ 4.88 > 3.5**, the colour difference between the sample and the reference standard is **clearly perceptible** to the average human observer. The juice shows higher redness (+2.5) and yellowness (+3.2) but lower lightness (−2.7) compared to the standard, indicating it is **darker, redder, and more yellow** — possibly an over-ripe or over-processed batch. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** What is **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)**? With a neat labelled force–time diagram, explain how **Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Gumminess, and Chewiness** are determined instrumentally. (7 marks) **(b)** A sauce exhibits **power-law behaviour** with consistency index $K = 12 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ and flow behaviour index $n = 0.45$. It is sheared at $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$. Calculate the **shear stress**, the **apparent viscosity**, and comment on whether this is a **shear-thinning** or **shear-thickening** fluid. (7 marks) --- ##### **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition (1 mark):** Texture Profile Analysis (TPA), developed by **Bourne (1978)**, is an instrumental double-compression test that imitates the human two-bite mastication cycle. A probe is driven into a food sample twice in succession while force, distance, and time are recorded, yielding a force–time curve from which seven textural parameters are derived. **Force–time curve (2 marks) — Diagram to be drawn:** ``` Force (N) ▲ │ H (Hardness) │ ╱╲ │ ╱ ╲ H₂ │ ╱ ╲ ╱╲ │ ╱ A1 ╲ ╱ ╲ │ ╱ ╲ ╱ A2 ╲ │ ╱ ╲╱ ╲___ │ ╱ ← Adhesiveness→ │ ╱ withdrawal ╱ │ ╱ ╱ Springiness (S) │ ╱ ╱ ───► │╱____________________╱_________________► Time (s) └─Compression 1─ Pause ─Compression 2─ ``` **Parameter definitions (4 marks):** | Parameter | Definition | How measured from curve | |---|---|---| | **Hardness (H)** | Peak force of first compression | Vertical height of first peak | | **Cohesiveness (C)** | Ratio of work in second bite to first | $C = A_2 / A_1$ (dimensionless, 0–1) | | **Springiness (S)** | Distance recovered between bites | Horizontal distance at force zero between bites | | **Adhesiveness (A)** | Negative work during withdrawal | Area of negative force loop | | **Gumminess (G)** | Energy required to disintegrate a semi-solid | $G = H \times C$ | | **Chewiness (Ch)** | Energy for chewing a solid | $Ch = G \times S = H \times C \times S$ | --- ##### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - $K = 12 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ - $n = 0.45$ - $\dot{\gamma} = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$ **Step 1 — Shear stress using power-law model:** $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ $$\tau = 12 \cdot (50)^{0.45}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $(50)^{0.45}$:** $$\ln(50) = 3.912$$ $$0.45 \times 3.912 = 1.7604$$ $$e^{1.7604} = 5.815$$ So $(50)^{0.45} \approx 5.815$ $$\tau = 12 \times 5.815 = 69.78 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Apparent viscosity:** $$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{69.78}{50} = 1.396 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** Since $n = 0.45 < 1$, the fluid is a **shear-thinning pseudoplastic** fluid. This means: - As shear rate increases, viscosity decreases. - At low shear (rest), the sauce appears thick (high $\eta_{app}$). - At high shear (squeezing bottle, stirring), it flows easily. - Typical examples: ketchup, mustard, salad dressings, banana purée. **Confirmation by apparent viscosity behaviour:** if we had computed at a higher $\dot{\gamma}$, say 200 s⁻¹, $\eta_{app}$ would drop to: $$\tau = 12 \times 200^{0.45} = 12 \times 11.43 = 137.2 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\eta_{app} = 137.2 / 200 = 0.686 \text{ Pa·s} < 1.396 \text{ Pa·s} \;\;\checkmark$$ This drop confirms **shear-thinning**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign errors in Δa\* and Δb\*** — Always subtract REFERENCE from SAMPLE consistently. Reversing the order changes ΔE sign but not magnitude — but writing `ΔL = 45.0 − 42.3 = +2.7` is a **1-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. > 2. **Hue angle quadrant mistake** — `tan⁻¹(b*/a*)` gives an angle in only ONE quadrant. KTU expects students to note when `a* < 0` and add 180°, or when `a* < 0 and b* < 0` add 360°. Skipping this loses **1 mark**. > 3. **Mixing up units** — Viscosity is **Pa·s**, NOT **Pa** (which is shear stress). Confusing them loses **1–2 marks**. > 4. **TPA Gumminess** — A common error is computing Gumminess = $H + C$ instead of $H \times C$. This is a **2-mark deduction** as it shows conceptual misunderstanding. > 5. **Pycnometer formula memorization** — Students often forget the denominator `(m3 − m1) − (m4 − m2)` and incorrectly write `(m3 − m1) + (m4 − m2)`. Lose **1 mark**. > 6. **Forgetting to state temperature** — Specific gravity is temperature-dependent. Reporting SG without stating "at 20 °C" or "at 25 °C" loses **0.5 mark**. > 7. **Power-law units** — Students write $K$ in units of Pa·sⁿ without realizing $n$ depends on K's exponent; forgetting the units makes answer technically wrong. Lose **0.5–1 mark**. > 8. **ΔE perceptibility scale** — Students often state ΔE = 4.88 is "not perceptible" — WRONG. Memorize: <1 not, 1–2 barely, 2–3.5 noticeable, >3.5 clearly visible. Lose **1 mark**. --- ### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density bullet recap — read this 5 minutes before the exam!** #### **A. Colour Measurement** - [ ] CIELAB uses D65 illuminant + 10° observer (most common) or 2°. - [ ] L\* = 0 (black) to 100 (white). - [ ] a\* positive = red, negative = green. - [ ] b\* positive = yellow, negative = blue. - [ ] ΔE\*ab = √[(ΔL)² + (Δa)² + (Δb)²]. - [ ] Chroma = √(a² + b²); Hue = tan⁻¹(b\*/a\*). - [ ] Munsell system uses Hue + Value + Chroma (H V/C). - [ ] Whiteness Index uses only L\* and b\*; Browning Index for bakery. #### **B. Viscosity Measurement** - [ ] η = τ / γ̇ (Newton's law). - [ ] Kinematic ν = η / ρ. - [ ] Ostwald/Ubbelohde use Hagen-Poiseuille equation. - [ ] Brookfield rotational gives shear rate sweep → flow curve. - [ ] Power-law: τ = K γ̇ⁿ. - [ ] Newtonian: n = 1. Pseudoplastic: n < 1. Dilatant: n > 1. - [ ] Bingham plastic has yield stress τ₀. - [ ] Casson model fits chocolate. - [ ] SI unit is **Pa·s**; **1 cP = 1 mPa·s**. #### **C. Texture Measurement** - [ ] TPA = two-bite simulation (Bourne 1978). - [ ] Hardness = peak force of 1st compression. - [ ] Cohesiveness = A2/A1. - [ ] Springiness = distance recovered. - [ ] Gumminess = Hardness × Cohesiveness. - [ ] Chewiness = Gumminess × Springiness = H × C × S. - [ ] Adhesiveness = negative area. - [ ] Resilience = upstroke/downstroke area ratio. - [ ] Warner-Bratzler shear for meat toughness. - [ ] Three-point bend for brittle foods (cookies, chips). #### **D. Specific Gravity Measurement** - [ ] SG = ρ_substance / ρ_water (at 4 °C reference). - [ ] Pycnometer most accurate for viscous/expensive samples. - [ ] Hydrometer (lactometer, saccharometer) for liquids. - [ ] Digital density meter uses oscillating U-tube. - [ ] Brix ≈ % sucrose; refractometer measures °Brix. - [ ] Density cup for paints/sauces; not as accurate. - [ ] Always report temperature (typically 20 °C or 25 °C). #### **E. Rheological Characteristics** - [ ] Viscoelastic behavior combines solid (elastic) + liquid (viscous). - [ ] G' = storage modulus (elastic energy stored). - [ ] G'' = loss modulus (viscous energy dissipated). - [ ] tan δ = G''/G'. - [ ] Gel: G' > G'' (δ → 0). - [ ] Liquid: G'' > G' (δ → 90°). - [ ] Gel point: G' = G'' (δ = 45°). - [ ] Creep test: J(t) = γ(t)/τ₀ compliance. - [ ] Models: Maxwell (relaxation), Kelvin-Voigt (creep), Burger's (combined). #### **F. Exam Strategy Quick Tips** - [ ] Always write **units** next to numerical answers. - [ ] State **assumptions** (laminar flow, isothermal, Newtonian/non-Newtonian). - [ ] Draw **labelled diagrams** for TPA curves — these guarantee full marks. - [ ] Cite **CIE/ASTM/AOAC standards** when relevant — shows depth. - [ ] Convert all units to **SI** before substituting into formulas. - [ ] When in doubt, mention **CIE D65, 10° observer, ASTM D2196, Bourne 1978, AOAC 920.212** — these are KTU-favourite reference codes. --- **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — INSTRUMENTAL MEASUREMENT OF SENSORY ATTRIBUTES**
